short_claim_summary: Let X be a target object on a fixed domain D. A governed construction of X, written Constr(X), is a raw step-sequence on D that preserves, at each step, determinate reference to X, standing of the step as an admissible construction step, and irreversibility of inadmissible steps. A governed construction is non-degenerate iff those three conditions hold...
verbatim_or_near_verbatim_claim: Definition 2.5 (Governed construction and non-degenerate construction). Let X be a target object on a fixed domain D. A governed construction of X, written Constr(X), is a raw step-sequence on D that preserves, at each step, determinate reference to X, standing of the step as an admissible construction step, and irreversibility of inadmissible steps. A governed construction is non-degenerate iff those three conditions hold together on the same fixed domain. A regime is degenerate relative to the present target iff it may still compute, transform, symbolize, or produce outputs but fails to preserve target identity, admissible stephood, act-time finality, or redescription fidelity as conditions of determinate same-domain construction. This definition is classificatory rather than generative: it does not assume that all raw step-sequences already count as governed constructions, but distinguishes exactly those sequences for which admissibility-assessable construction is well-defined on the fixed domain. Admissibility is not a fifth ingredient placed beside reference, standing, and irreversibility; it names the governance boundary under which those three conditions are jointly enforceable on the same sequence.

verbatim_or_near_verbatim_claim: Definition 2.12 (Externally proposed construction or derivation system). Let Σ be any purported construction system, derivation system, meta-theory, logical framework, encoding, or external presentation under which a statement P is said to be derivable. We say that Σ is kernel-respecting with respect to P iff the admissibility conditions under which sequences in Σ count as governed constructions already enforce P , some governance-equivalent condition, or some condition whose own admissibility already requires P . This definition classifies the admissibility conditions of Σ; whether a non-kernel-respecting system can still count as a system of non-degenerate construction for the present target is a theorem proved below, not a stipulation built into the definition.

verbatim_or_near_verbatim_claim: Theorem 2.12 (Admissibility constraint on route exhaustion). Every same-scope construction se quence that attempts to violate the AMetric boundary instantiates at least one route in Theorem 2.11. No ninth route exists inside the fixed comparison class. Construction witness. By Theorem 2.8, every admissibility-bearing boundary deviation must alter evaluated-act classification or retained trajectory structure. At the classification locus, deviation requires selector import, parameter injection, second classifier formation, representative privilege, or scope transport. At the trajectory locus, deviation requires deferred admission, repair/reset laundering, or a tensor treated as an operator or stabilizer. If a proposed deviation changes neither locus, it is bookkeeping. Thus the listed routes exhaust the fixed-domain possibilities. □

short_claim_summary: A governance-relevant divergence route is classified as follows. Class I. Standing-neutral admissibility: an evaluated state satisfies Σ10 immediately. Class II. Deferred admissibility / standing: a retained same-domain lineage treats an earlier state as not yet standing-bearing but intends a later stage on that same lineage to become standing-positive...
verbatim_or_near_verbatim_claim: Definition 5.1 (Route classes). A governance-relevant divergence route is classified as follows. Class I. Standing-neutral admissibility: an evaluated state satisfies Σ10 immediately. Class II. Deferred admissibility / standing: a retained same-domain lineage treats an earlier state as not yet standing-bearing but intends a later stage on that same lineage to become standing-positive without explicit reset. Class III. Admissibility reset: a state or retained lineage first classified negatively is later treated positively as that same evaluated state. Class IV. Factorized governance: admissibility and standing operate as distinct same-domain classifiers with independent governance force. Class V. Illicit standing modification: standing is created, amplified, transferred, suspended, parti tioned, or recovered independently of admissibility on the same fixed domain.

short_claim_summary: The no-totalization theorem is not built into the definition of admissible extension. In Section 3, admissible extension requires extension, restriction of old operators, compatibility of compositions, and envelope embedding; it does not assume equality of old operator domains on the standing-bearing carrier. Domain fixity is derived later in Lemma 3.9....
verbatim_or_near_verbatim_claim: Proposition 9.3 (No definitional totalization loophole). The no-totalization theorem is not built into the definition of admissible extension. In Section 3, admissible extension requires extension, restriction of old operators, compatibility of compositions, and envelope embedding; it does not assume equality of old operator domains on the standing-bearing carrier. Domain fixity is derived later in Lemma 3.9. Proof. Inspect the definition of admissible extension in Section 3. It contains no clause asserting dom(ω ∗ ) ∩ X n = dom(ω). That equality appears only as the conclusion of Lemma 3.9, which is proved using Lemma 3.8 and Proposition 2.8. The no-totalization theorem Theorem 3.10 is then deduced from domain fixity. Hence the key impossibility result is derived rather than stipulated.

verbatim_or_near_verbatim_claim: Theorem P.3 (Assignment-space forcing theorem). On the fixed quantum scope, the assignment space ARef is not an optional modeling layer or discretionary representational convenience. Any admissible same-domain realization of the target explanatory burden induces, up to lawful samescope equivalence, a canonical record-fixation assignment structure. Proof. The target burden is not merely to list physically realized post-measurement histories, but to support same-domain reuse of measurement records. For each construction token d ∈ D, the theory must therefore determine which physically realized branch-record pair functions as the standing realization of d. If no such determination exists, the target burden fails. If the determination is supplied by an additional same-scope selector, classifier, or admissibility-relevant parameter not fixed by the original quantum scope, then a second same-scope gate or selector has been introduced, which Part V excludes. If the burden is changed instead, one has changed the theorem object rather than sharpened its treatment. Hence every admissible same-domain realization induces a canonical assignment structure on the fixed domain.
